pain_point_label: Late or Missed Identification of Learning Differences

matches.match_rationale: Early warning signs — fine motor delays, speech delays, poor phonemic awareness, attendance patterns, assessment scores — are all structured or semi-structured data already captured in student information systems. A classification model trained on these feature vectors can flag students in Grades 1-2 who exhibit risk profiles consistent with dyslexia, ADHD, or other learning differences, without requiring a diagnostician on site. This directly addresses the core pain mechanism: absence of a systematic screening protocol that can operate at scale across rural and urban campuses.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A universal early-screening model that ingests existing SIS data (grades, teacher ratings, attendance, assessment sub-scores) each term and outputs a risk flag per student, triggering a standardized teacher checklist before Grade 2 ends. Deployable as a dashboard layer on top of existing student data exports.

matches.match_rationale: The problem is precisely about adverse outcomes — 2-3 years of unidentified learning differences leading to compounded academic failure — which maps directly to risk stratification. A risk score per student, derived from early academic and behavioral signals, gives SEN coordinators a ranked watchlist rather than waiting for reading gaps to become visible. This is especially high-value for campuses with only one part-time psychometrician: triage by AI-generated risk score means the limited specialist sees the highest-need students first.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A per-student SEN risk score updated each semester, surfaced in a coordinator dashboard, with configurable alert thresholds so rural campuses without on-site specialists can prioritize referrals to traveling diagnosticians or telehealth psychologists.

matches.match_rationale: Class teachers at rural campuses lack training in early SEN identification. A conversational agent can guide teachers through a structured developmental screening interview — asking about specific classroom behaviors, providing examples, and collecting structured responses — replicating the intake process a trained psychometrician would run. This directly addresses the 'absence of trained screeners' causal factor without requiring specialist hiring at every campus.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A teacher-facing chat assistant that walks through a validated early screening protocol (e.g., adapted DIBELS or universal screener questions) for flagged or teacher-nominated students, outputs a structured referral summary, and routes it to the SEN coordinator for follow-up.

matches.match_rationale: The absence of a universal screening protocol means that even when concern is raised, the referral-to-assessment pipeline is manual, slow, and inconsistent across campuses. A task automation agent can orchestrate the end-to-end workflow: flagging students based on risk model output, notifying class teachers, triggering a screening checklist, routing completed checklists to the SEN coordinator, scheduling specialist review, and logging outcomes — eliminating the administrative gaps that cause cases to fall through.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated SEN referral pipeline that triggers on risk-flag or teacher nomination, routes tasks to the right person at each step, tracks SLA (e.g., screening completed within 10 school days), and escalates overdue referrals — replacing the current ad-hoc email/paper process.
